quote: Humans can write summaries without hallucination; LLMs cannot do so reliably.
claim: LLMs cannot reliably write summaries without hallucination, unlike humans.

quote: Humans can learn rules of games like chess from modest amounts of explicit instruction; LLMs can't stick to explicit rules.
claim: LLMs cannot reliably follow explicit rules, unlike humans who can learn game rules from modest instruction.

quote: we still have a long way to go on all the things I always harp on: reasoning, compositionality, planning, factuality, and so on
claim: AI still has a long way to go on reasoning, compositionality, planning, and factuality.

quote: Dropping from "as smart as the Star Trek computer" to better than Joe Sixpack should not be our goal.
claim: The standard for AGI should not be lowered to merely beating the average untrained human; AGI should reliably do what it is asked.

quote: What would make artificial general intelligence general is generality: the ability to cope with new things. And that's precisely where current AI is still lacking.
claim: Current AI lacks the generality -- the ability to cope with novel situations -- that would define true AGI.

quote: Let's not dumb down our standards, and wind up with a bunch of machines we can't rely on, just so that big tech can prematurely declare victory, when there is plenty of work left to do.
claim: The AI industry should not lower AGI standards to allow premature victory declarations by big tech.

trigger: arXiv paper on building conscious machines
quote: We can't even control LLMs. Do we really want to open another, perhaps even riskier box?
claim: We cannot even control LLMs, so pursuing the construction of conscious machines opens a potentially even riskier endeavor.

quote: In less than a year, ChatGPT has gone from being mistaken for AGI to being the butt of a joke, and an insulting shorthand for robotic, incoherent, unreliable, and untrustworthy.
claim: In less than a year, ChatGPT went from being mistaken for AGI to being a shorthand for robotic, incoherent, unreliable, and untrustworthy output.

quote: a lot of companies are struggling to actually deploy generative AI, "due to high costs and confusion"
claim: Many companies are struggling to deploy generative AI due to high costs and confusion.

quote: Some of the problems we've known about for a long time appear to persist. Google's latest LLM "includes Hitler, Stalin and Mussolini on a list of "greatest" leaders"
claim: Known problems with LLMs persist; Google's latest LLM still produces outputs like listing dictators as greatest leaders.

quote: Large Language Models aren't like classical databases in which individual pieces of data can be removed at will; if any content is removed, the entire model must (so far as I understand it) be retrained, at great expense.
claim: Individual training data cannot be selectively removed from LLMs; removing content requires retraining the entire model at great expense.

quote: Personally, despite the name of this essay, I am not all that worried about humans going extinct anytime very soon. It's hard for me to construct a realistic scenario of literal, full-on extinction that I take seriously.
claim: Human extinction from AI in the near term is unlikely; it is hard to construct a realistic scenario for complete extinction.

quote: p(catastrophe) – the chance that an incident that kills (say) one percent or more of the population, seems a lot higher
claim: The probability of an AI-related catastrophe (killing 1%+ of the population) is significantly higher than the probability of full extinction.

quote: None of this means of course that I suddenly think that we are close to super-smart machines. (Spoiler alert: we are not).
claim: We are not close to building super-intelligent machines.

quote: When I think about how poor his company (Meta) is even at making LLMs that don't hallucinate, I get worried.
claim: Meta is poor at making LLMs that don't hallucinate.

quote: I am, at least for now, much more worried about the overapplication of mediocre AI (eg what happens if we put large language models into running military strategy or into hiring decisions that affect millions of people) than the use of AI that might be so smart it could catch its own errors.
claim: The overapplication of mediocre AI to high-stakes domains like military strategy and hiring is a greater near-term risk than superintelligent AI.

quote: The risks of current AI (bias, defamation, cybercrime, wholesale disinformation, etc) are already starting to be well documented and may themselves quickly escalate, and could lead to geopolitical instability even over the next few years.
claim: Current AI risks (bias, defamation, cybercrime, disinformation) are already documented and could escalate to cause geopolitical instability in the next few years.

quote: there is a good chance that any immediate effects will disproportionately affect the dispossessed
claim: The immediate negative effects of AI will disproportionately affect disadvantaged populations.

quote: there is no solid argument that the number is zero, or even that the risk is infinitesimal
claim: There is no solid argument that the probability of AI-caused doom or catastrophe is zero or infinitesimal.

trigger: DHS AI surveillance tool report by 404 Media
quote: I don't know what's worse. The fact that the US is apparently using these tools, or the fact that they aren't likely to be reliable, almost certainly regularly reporting false alarms, and likely full of insidious bias, like so many previous AI systems have shown themselves to be?
claim: AI surveillance tools used by the US government are likely unreliable, regularly producing false alarms and containing insidious bias.

quote: What I am sure of, though, is that our chances to avoid serious consequences aren't great if we control neither AI nor the companies that make AI. The goal of the companies is return to shareholders, not to return to humanity.
claim: Our chances of avoiding serious AI consequences are poor if we do not regulate AI and AI companies, whose goal is shareholder return rather than societal benefit.

quote: The default right now is regulatory capture: the companies will make the rules, telling the governments what they are and are not comfortable with.
claim: The current default trajectory for AI regulation is regulatory capture, where AI companies dictate the rules to governments.

quote: we have transparency in name, but not in deed. Without data transparency, we can't make sure our models are without bias, or even know the sources of the biases they hide within
claim: AI companies have committed to transparency in name only; without actual data transparency, we cannot identify or mitigate bias in AI models.

quote: If we are to get the right regulatory regime for AI – one that protects consumers, without stifling innovation – we are going to need government to step up.
claim: Government must actively step up to create an AI regulatory regime that protects consumers without stifling innovation.

quote: what Lenat tried to do - to get machines to represent and reason about common sense — still must be done
claim: Getting machines to represent and reason about common sense, as Cyc attempted, is still an unsolved and necessary goal for AI.

quote: Depending on the wording, any given model might or might not get questions like that right on any given day; Large Language Models tend to be accurate in some wordings, inaccurate in others. From them we sometimes see an illusion of common sense, depending on the vagaries of what the training set is and the precise wording of a question, but something is obviously still missing.
claim: LLMs produce an illusion of common sense that varies with wording and training set coverage, rather than genuine understanding.

quote: One can imagine an alternative AI paradigm that could learn much of what it needed to know to be effective in the world without going through all your personal data, but that does not yet exist.
claim: An AI paradigm that could be effective without consuming users' personal data does not yet exist.

quote: The paradigm we have right now is so data-greedy that the entire public internet doesn't seem to be enough. Your private data are going to feed the insatiable beast, too — like it or not.
claim: The current AI paradigm is so data-hungry that training on the entire public internet is insufficient, driving companies to harvest private user data as well.

trigger: six-month anniversary of the pause letter
quote: Large language models continue to be unreliable. None of the basic issues that I have been harping on for the last 20 years has actually been solved. Large language models still make stuff up ("hallucinate"), still can't be trusted in their reasoning, and remain unreliable.
claim: LLMs continue to be unreliable: they still hallucinate, cannot be trusted in their reasoning, and none of the basic reliability issues have been solved.

trigger: six-month anniversary of the pause letter
quote: GPT-5–which is essentially the only thing that the pause letter (if you read it carefully) actually proposed to pause—does actually seem to be on pause
claim: GPT-5 training appears to be on pause, consistent with Altman's May 2023 Senate testimony that OpenAI was not training it and had no plans to in the next six months.

trigger: six-month anniversary of the pause letter
quote: At least in the United States, policy around AI is still largely notional, not actual. Voluntary guidelines aren't laws, and they aren't enforcement mechanisms, either; companies can still do as they please.
claim: US AI policy is still largely notional; voluntary guidelines are not laws and provide no enforcement, leaving companies free to do as they please.

trigger: six-month anniversary of the pause letter
quote: The big companies have all pledged fealty to transparency, but none of them will actually tells us what's in their data sets.
claim: Major AI companies have pledged transparency but none actually disclose the contents of their training datasets.

trigger: six-month anniversary of the pause letter
quote: society is placing more and more trust in a technology that simply has not yet earned that trust
claim: Society is placing increasing trust in LLM technology that has not yet earned that trust through demonstrated reliability.

trigger: six-month anniversary of the pause letter
quote: As a society, we still don't really have any plan whatsoever about what we might do to mitigate long-term risks.
claim: Society has no plan for mitigating long-term AI risks such as superintelligent AI or AI-enabled bioweapons.

trigger: Berglund et al. reversal curse paper
quote: can LLMs trained on A is B infer automatically that B is A? The shocking (yet, in historical context, see below, unsurprising) answer is no
claim: LLMs trained on 'A is B' cannot automatically infer that 'B is A', demonstrating a fundamental failure in relational reasoning (the reversal curse).

trigger: Berglund et al. reversal curse paper
quote: The core problem, as I pointed out then, is that in many real world problems, you can never fully cover the space of possible examples, and in a broad class of heavily-data-driven systems like LLMs that lack explicit variables and operations over variables, you are out of luck when you try to extrapolate beyond that space of training examples.
claim: LLMs lack explicit variables and operations over variables, which prevents them from extrapolating beyond the space of training examples on many real-world problems.

trigger: Berglund et al. reversal curse paper
quote: Forty one years later, neural networks (at least of the popular variety) still struggle with this. They still remain pointillistic masses of blurry memory, never as systematic as reasoning machines ought to be.
claim: Neural networks remain pointillistic masses of blurry memory, still struggling with systematic reasoning over 40 years after Fodor and Pylyshyn identified the problem.

trigger: Berglund et al. reversal curse paper
quote: The will to believe in neural networks is frequently so strong that counterevidence is often dismissed or ignored, for much too long.
claim: Counterevidence against neural network capabilities is frequently dismissed or ignored due to strong will to believe in them.

trigger: Berglund et al. reversal curse paper
quote: It's just a matter before people start to realize that we need some genuinely new ideas in the field, either new mechanisms (perhaps neurosymbolic), or different approaches altogether.
claim: The AI field needs genuinely new ideas, such as neurosymbolic mechanisms or entirely different approaches, to overcome LLM limitations.

trigger: OpenAI employee share sale at $90B valuation
quote: There is a ton of pending litigation, with multiple lawsuits from artists and writers, and rumors of a major NYT lawsuit coming.
claim: OpenAI faces significant legal risk from pending litigation by artists and writers, with potentially massive damages for copyright infringement.

trigger: OpenAI employee share sale at $90B valuation
quote: There's not a huge moat here to protect OpenAI from competitors. The central technology at OpenAI (as far as anyone knows) is large language models, and many other companies know how to build them.
claim: OpenAI lacks a strong competitive moat because many other companies know how to build LLMs and some are open-sourcing competing models.

trigger: OpenAI employee share sale at $90B valuation
quote: Big customers are still skittish; lots of people are trying LLMs, but JP Morgan, Apple, and others have bans on their use, at least partly motivated around concerns about data leaks, but reliability is a problem too.
claim: Major enterprises like JP Morgan and Apple have banned LLM use due to concerns about data leaks and reliability issues.

trigger: OpenAI employee share sale at $90B valuation
quote: LLMs will likely never deliver on the promise that they are somehow close to AGI.
claim: LLMs will likely never deliver on the promise that they are close to AGI.

trigger: OpenAI employee share sale at $90B valuation
quote: Even now, GPT can't be trusted to follow the rules of chess, for example, and those rules are stated directly (probably multiple times) in the training set.
claim: GPT cannot be trusted to reliably follow the rules of chess despite those rules being explicitly stated in its training data.

trigger: OpenAI employee share sale at $90B valuation
quote: I have no doubt, of course, that some AI companies will eventually merit these stratospheric valuations, but I am not convinced that a pure LLM play ever will.
claim: A pure LLM company may never merit the stratospheric valuations being assigned to companies like OpenAI.

trigger: Bing fabricating Speaker of the House election results
quote: I fully stand by my claims. Rumors that LLMs have solved then hallucination problem are greatly exaggerated.
claim: Claims that LLMs have solved the hallucination problem are greatly exaggerated.

trigger: Bing fabricating Speaker of the House election results
quote: This all happens not because LLMs are smart, in the sense of being able to compare what they say with the world, but simply because they are good at making new sentences that sound old sentences, the essence of autocomplete.
claim: LLMs generate false information because they produce sentences that sound like previously seen text rather than comparing outputs against the world.

trigger: Bing fabricating Speaker of the House election results
quote: The worst part is not that every single sentence contains at least one lie, but that the whole thing sounds detailed and real. To someone who wasn't following matters closely, it might well all sound plausible.
claim: LLM hallucinations are especially dangerous because they sound detailed and plausible, making them likely to deceive people who are not closely following the relevant facts.

trigger: Gurnee and Tegmark 'Language Models Represent Space and Time' paper
quote: correlations aren't causal, semantic models
claim: LLMs finding correlations with spatial and temporal features does not mean they have genuine causal or semantic models of space and time.

trigger: Gurnee and Tegmark 'Language Models Represent Space and Time' paper
quote: The whole point of models is to be able to use them downstream for other purposes, e,g, in robotics, navigation, computer graphics, and so on. Models that can't be used downstream are scarcely worthy of the name
claim: Statistical correlations in LLMs that cannot be used downstream for inference, navigation, or other tasks do not constitute genuine world models.

trigger: Gurnee and Tegmark 'Language Models Represent Space and Time' paper
quote: Pretty much every fantasy I have ever heard about GPT-5 doesn't reckon with that fact, the fact that the correlational regime doesn't yield flexible models that can reliably support inferences on novel questions.
claim: The correlational regime underlying LLMs does not yield flexible models that can reliably support inferences on novel questions, undermining expectations for GPT-5.

trigger: Gurnee and Tegmark 'Language Models Represent Space and Time' paper
quote: safety, which is what Tegmark really cares about, is not going to emerge anytime soon, and probably ever, from hit-or-miss systems that substitute correlation for a factual, structured, interrogable representations of the world
claim: AI safety will not emerge from systems that substitute statistical correlation for factual, structured, interrogable representations of the world.

trigger: Gurnee and Tegmark 'Language Models Represent Space and Time' paper
quote: AI is unlikely to ever be trustworthy if its makers continue to skip the step of having explicit cognitive models of the world
claim: AI is unlikely to become trustworthy without incorporating explicit cognitive models of the world.

quote: I don't we are all that close to machines that are more intelligent than us, I don't think they really understand the things that they say, and I don't think they are intelligent in the sense of being able to adaptively and flexibly reason about things they haven't encountered before, in a reliable way.
claim: Current AI systems are not close to being more intelligent than humans, do not genuinely understand what they say, and cannot adaptively and flexibly reason about novel situations.

quote: a lot of what we are seeing now is a kind of unreliable mimicry
claim: Much of what LLMs produce is a form of unreliable mimicry rather than genuine intelligence or understanding.

quote: The connections in chatbots are very different from the connections in the brain; it's a mistake to compare them directly in this way.
claim: It is a mistake to directly compare the connections in chatbots to connections in the human brain, as they are fundamentally different (e.g., neuron type diversity).

quote: If you have a large enough database, you can do a half decent job just by looking up the most similar sentence in the database, and saying what was said in that context. Large language models are trained, as far as we know, on pretty much the entire internet. That gives them enormous databases to train on, and means that the feat of prediction doesn't necessarily tell us anything about understanding.
claim: Good next-word prediction does not necessarily imply understanding; with a large enough training database (like the entire internet), reasonable predictions can emerge from pattern matching alone.

quote: Baby shoes never worn is a very old story, sometimes attributed to Hemingway, with about 21 million Google hits, and an entire wikipedia entry, as perhaps the best known example of very short fiction. I am floored that you didn't bother to check if the story was original. Your best example of a spectacular machine invention is in fact a perfect example of the kind of parroting and theft of intellectual property that is characteristic of large language models.
claim: The 60 Minutes 'baby shoes' example presented as creative AI output is actually parroting of a famous piece of very short fiction, exemplifying LLMs' tendency to reproduce rather than create.

quote: wholesale, automated fake news will be used both to manipulate markets and elections, and might undermine democracy
claim: Automated AI-generated fake news will be used to manipulate markets and elections and may undermine democracy.

quote: I am much more worried about bad actors deliberately misusing AI, than machines deliberately wanting to take over.
claim: Deliberate misuse of AI by bad actors is a more likely threat than machines autonomously wanting to take over.

quote: In 2016 you said that it was "quite obvious that we should stop training radiologists" because deep learning was getting so good. You know how many radiologists have been replaced by machines seven years later? Zero.
claim: Despite Hinton's 2016 prediction that radiologist training should stop due to deep learning advances, zero radiologists have been replaced by AI seven years later.

quote: The problem here is not that DALL-E 3 is not trying to be racist. It's that it can't separate the world from the statistics of its dataset.
claim: DALL-E 3 cannot separate its outputs from the statistical biases in its training dataset, leading to racially biased image generation.

quote: DALL-E does not have a cognitive construct of a doctor or a patient or a human being or an occupation or medicine or race or egalitarianism or equal opportunity or any of that.
claim: DALL-E lacks genuine cognitive constructs for concepts like doctor, patient, race, or egalitarianism; it operates purely from statistical patterns.

quote: Blind fealty to the statistics of arbitrary data is not AGI. It's a hack. And not one that we should want.
claim: Blindly following the statistics of training data is a hack, not AGI, and is not a desirable approach for AI systems.

quote: we need to "invent then a new breed of AI systems that mix an awareness of the past with values that represent the future that we aspire to. Our focus should be on figuring on how to build AI that can represent and reason about *values*, rather than simply perpetuating past data"
claim: AI systems need to be built that can represent and reason about human values, rather than simply perpetuating patterns from past data.

quote: success there is not because the network knows how to extract meanings from individual words ("astronaut", "riding" and so forth) or to combine them into semantic meanings based on their syntax (which is what linguist compositionality is all about), but rather that the network does something more holistic and approximate, a bit like keyword matching and a lot less like deep language understanding.
claim: Image generation models like DALL-E succeed through holistic approximate keyword matching rather than genuine compositional language understanding.

quote: being long-term optimistic about technology doesn't mean you have to ignore the short-term (or long-term) risks of technology
claim: Genuine techno-optimism requires recognizing and working to address technology risks, not ignoring them.

trigger: The Information reporting OpenAI's Arrakis project cancelled
quote: it's never been clear to me that simply building a bigger model which would actually solve any of those problems
claim: Simply building bigger language models is unlikely to solve the fundamental problems of unreliability, bias, and hallucination.

trigger: The Information reporting OpenAI's Arrakis project cancelled
quote: Still a third theory, my own, is that by the time of Altman's remarks in May, OpenAI had already run some tests as proof of concept, and didn't like what they saw. They might have decided that GPT-5, if it was simply a bigger version of GPT-4, would not meet expectations
claim: OpenAI likely delayed GPT-5 because internal proof-of-concept tests showed that a simply scaled-up GPT-4 would not meet expectations.

trigger: The Information reporting OpenAI's Arrakis project cancelled
quote: If Yann LeCun and I are correct, AI will need genuinely new paradigms to get to the next level in AI, and OpenAI may be no closer to that than anyone else.
claim: AI will need genuinely new paradigms beyond current LLM approaches to reach the next level, and OpenAI may have no special advantage in discovering them.

trigger: The Information reporting OpenAI's Arrakis project cancelled
quote: If GPT-5 did fail to please, or even if it were just deferred indefinitely (like so many promises that have been about driverless cars), it could have a rapid, deflationary effect on sky-high valuations.
claim: A disappointing or indefinitely deferred GPT-5 could rapidly deflate the sky-high valuations in the AI industry.

trigger: Agüera y Arcas and Norvig 'AGI Is Already Here' article
quote: by the classical criteria for AGI [Artificial General Intelligence], LLM's hardly qualify
claim: By classical criteria for AGI, current LLMs do not qualify as artificial general intelligence.

trigger: Agüera y Arcas and Norvig 'AGI Is Already Here' article
quote: They can't count, do five digit multiplication, reliably generalize kinship relations, or even reliably follow the rules of chess, and so on—despite the fact that each of these activities is presumably well explained, explicitly, in the training set
claim: Current LLMs cannot reliably count, do five-digit multiplication, generalize kinship relations, or follow the rules of chess, despite these being well explained in their training data.

trigger: Agüera y Arcas and Norvig 'AGI Is Already Here' article
quote: the boldfaced (written in bold pullout text) claim that "The most important parts of AGI have already been achieved by the current generation of advanced AI large language models" is logically flawed. Until we get to the finish line, we can't know the route.
claim: The claim that the most important parts of AGI have already been achieved is logically flawed because we cannot know which capabilities are most important until AGI is actually achieved.

trigger: Agüera y Arcas and Norvig 'AGI Is Already Here' article
quote: true AGI might revolve around planning with respect to detailed world models that are maintained over time; as Subbarao Kambhampati has shown, LLMs are weak at planning; as hallucinations show, they are also weak at the maintenance of world models, strongly suggesting some key ingredients have not yet been invented
claim: LLMs are weak at planning and world model maintenance, strongly suggesting that key ingredients for AGI have not yet been invented.

trigger: Agüera y Arcas and Norvig 'AGI Is Already Here' article
quote: for many different AI tasks --- robotics, image interpretation, planning, game playing, scientific applications, many forms of reasoning --- state of the art AI currently makes little to no use of the transformer architecture that underpins LLMs
claim: State-of-the-art AI for robotics, image interpretation, planning, game playing, scientific applications, and many forms of reasoning makes little to no use of the transformer architecture underpinning LLMs.

trigger: Agüera y Arcas and Norvig 'AGI Is Already Here' article
quote: In AI, no single technique is likely to be a silver bullet, now or ever.
claim: No single AI technique is likely to be a silver bullet for achieving general intelligence.

quote: the LLM based system is generalizing by similarity, doing better on cases that are in or near the training set, never, ever getting to a complete, abstract, reliable representation of what multiplication is
claim: LLMs generalize multiplication by similarity to training examples rather than learning the abstract algorithm, and their performance degrades as problems diverge from the training set.

quote: even under carefully controlled circumstances with massive amounts of directly relevant data, they still never really get even the most basic linear functions
claim: Even with massive amounts of relevant training data and controlled conditions, LLMs fail to reliably learn basic mathematical functions like multiplication.

quote: the same Achilles' Heel remains
claim: The inability of neural networks to learn abstract, similarity-independent rules (as opposed to similarity-based patterns) is an Achilles' heel that has persisted for over 30 years.

quote: multimodal training is not magically making hallucinations go away. GPT-v (the visual version of GPT) can use words like refrigerator and counter, but that doesn't mean it actually knows what these things mean.
claim: Multimodal training (combining vision and language) does not solve the hallucination problem; multimodal LLMs still lack genuine understanding of the objects they label.

quote: The old systems were lost in a sea of correlations between words; the new system are lost in a sea of correlations between words and pixels.
claim: Multimodal LLMs have merely expanded from correlations between words to correlations between words and pixels, without achieving genuine understanding.

quote: large language models are a profound step backwards; they lack stable models of the world, which means that they are poor at planning, and unable to reason reliably; everything becomes hit or miss.
claim: In some ways LLMs are a step backwards from earlier AI: they lack stable world models, are poor at planning, and unable to reason reliably.

quote: no matter what investors and CEOs might tell you, hallucinations will remain inevitable
claim: LLM hallucinations will remain inevitable regardless of what investors and CEOs claim.

quote: this does not mean that GPT-4 can now reliably solve word problems that involve simple math or involve a combination of simple science and math; it cannot.
claim: GPT-4 with calculator and Wolfram Alpha plug-ins still cannot reliably solve word problems involving simple math or combinations of math and science.

quote: neither GPT-4 nor the plug-ins have the commonsense world knowledge and reasonable abilities needed to reliably translate a word problem into a mathematical calculation
claim: GPT-4 and its plug-ins lack the commonsense knowledge needed to reliably translate word problems into mathematical calculations.

quote: GPT-4 doesn't reliably understand how to use the tools
claim: GPT-4 does not reliably understand how to use external tools like Code Interpreter and Wolfram Alpha.

quote: Finding a way to get LLMs to reliably use external tools is far from trivial.
claim: Getting LLMs to reliably use external tools (calculators, fact-checkers, programming languages) is a far from trivial problem.

quote: It is natural for humans to imagine LLMs to be generally intelligent in all the ways that humans are, and perfectly capable of taking their own output and acting on them in all the ways that trained humans might be able to do. In reality, they are not able to do so.
claim: Humans naturally but incorrectly assume LLMs are generally intelligent in all the ways humans are; in reality, LLMs cannot reliably act on their own outputs the way trained humans can.

quote: We need research breakthroughs to solve some of today's technical challenges in creating AI with safe and ethical objectives. Some of these challenges are unlikely to be solved by simply making AI systems more capable.
claim: Research breakthroughs are needed for AI safety; simply making AI systems more capable will not solve current technical challenges in creating safe and ethical AI.

quote: some problems in AI might be too complex for individual labs, and not of sufficient financial interest to the large AI companies like Google and Facebook (now Meta), ehich are after all, driven by profits rather than humanity. That's still true.
claim: Some AI problems are too complex for individual labs and not financially interesting enough for large profit-driven tech companies to solve.

quote: while every large tech company is making some effort around AI safety at this point, the collective sum of those efforts hasn't yielded all that much.
claim: The collective AI safety efforts of large tech companies have not yielded significant results.

quote: We have no way of guaranteeing that large language models will reliably stick to facts
claim: There is currently no way to guarantee that large language models will reliably stick to facts.

quote: We have no way of guaranteeing that large language models won't be jailbroken into saying unethical things. To the contrary, a just-published technique shows that current LLMs can be jailbroken quickly, and automatically
claim: There is no way to guarantee LLMs cannot be jailbroken, and current LLMs can be jailbroken quickly and automatically.

quote: Current AI has no way to reliably anticipate consequences of actions that it might recommend
claim: Current AI has no reliable way to anticipate consequences of actions it might recommend.

quote: Anthropic's "Constitutional AI" idea seems to me to be clever and the best of the bunch, but still inadequate, riddled with the usual problems that LLMs face (reliability, excessive dependence on similarity to what is in the training set, hallucinations, unreliable reasoning, etc).
claim: Anthropic's Constitutional AI approach is inadequate due to the same fundamental problems all LLMs face: reliability issues, excessive dependence on training data similarity, hallucinations, and unreliable reasoning.

quote: the summit appears to be focusing primarily on long-term AI risk
claim: The UK AI Summit is too focused on long-term AI risk at the expense of current AI risks.

quote: you don't have to be superintelligent to create serious problems.
claim: AI does not need to be superintelligent to create serious problems; current non-AGI systems already pose significant risks.

quote: We need to stop worrying (just) about Skynet and robots taking over the world, and think a lot more about what criminals, including terrorists, might do with LLMs, and what, if anything, we might do to stop them.
claim: AI risk policy should focus more on criminals and terrorists misusing LLMs, not just on long-term existential risk scenarios.

quote: every day brings us closer to a world in which AI causes real harm.
claim: AI-caused real-world harm is increasing over time.

quote: There are over 60 elections around the world in 2024; the chance that at least one will be swayed by deep fakes is high.
claim: At least one of the 60+ elections worldwide in 2024 will likely be swayed by deepfakes.

quote: if Alphabet pulls the plug on Waymo, it may be the end of driverless cars for now– but not forever.
claim: If Waymo shuts down, it will cause a self-driving winter, but driverless cars will eventually succeed.

quote: I do still think that reliable self-driving cars will emerge, and eventually be safer than humans, who can get distracted, become tired and so forth.
claim: Reliable self-driving cars will eventually emerge and be safer than human drivers.

quote: To get there, though, what we need is a much smarter AI, a kind that can reason, and not hallucinate, grounded in reality and not just corpus statistics— which require fundamental research of a sort that is currently getting starved out in the LLM euphoria.
claim: Achieving reliable self-driving cars requires AI that can reason and not hallucinate, grounded in reality rather than corpus statistics, which requires fundamental research currently being starved out by LLM euphoria.

quote: The same by the way probably holds for generative search and personalized agents. We will eventually see search engines that return neatly written paragraphs, and eventually see AI agents that can organize lives. Or at least our grandchildren or their children will.
claim: Reliable generative search and personalized AI agents will eventually work, but may take generations to achieve.

quote: The sooner we recognize that there is life beyond current approaches to AI, the faster we will get there.
claim: Progress toward reliable AI applications requires moving beyond current deep learning approaches.

quote: How effective this actually becomes depends a lot on the exact wording, and how things are enforced, and how much of it is binding as opposed to merely voluntary.
claim: The effectiveness of the Biden AI Executive Order depends critically on enforcement and whether provisions are binding rather than voluntary.

quote: The Executive Order, as good as it is, may fall short. Companies will poke for loopholes and surely find them.
claim: AI companies will find and exploit loopholes in the Biden AI Executive Order.

quote: What we really need is a peer-review process by independent scientists; it still seems like the companies are free to do largely as we please. That's not enough.
claim: AI regulation needs an independent peer-review process by scientists, not just company self-reporting of risks.

quote: they only have to share results of internal testing, no matter how bad the risks might be. This is a long long way from the kind of FDA approval process that I advocated for here and in the Senate.
claim: The Executive Order's requirement to share internal testing results is insufficient compared to the FDA-style approval process needed for AI.

quote: Framing things as only pertaining to foundational models is plainly too narrow.
claim: Regulating only foundation models is too narrow because many AI applications (e.g., driverless cars) do not use foundation models.

quote: We the people need to speak up, and we can't let big tech call all the shots.
claim: AI governance is being dominated by big tech and needs greater public and civil society input.

quote: Nobody knows for SURE that there are no serious possible consequences of open source AI, e.g in terms of bioweapons, misinformation generation etc
claim: The safety consequences of open source AI (bioweapons, misinformation) are unknown and potentially serious.

quote: A giant company that is behind in the AI sweepstakes (at least with respect to public profile) is aggressively pushing open source AI, apparently as a way to raise its profile
claim: Meta is pushing open source AI primarily as a competitive strategy to raise its profile, not out of altruism.

quote: They have done this as quickly as possible, apparently without any external oversight whatsoever, without waiting for anything like formal results or scientific consensus.
claim: Meta released open source AI models without external oversight or waiting for scientific consensus on safety.

quote: The fact that a single company can unilaterally make this decision for all of humanity is terrifying.
claim: A single company should not be able to unilaterally release powerful open source AI models affecting all of humanity without oversight.

quote: Certainly no commercial product could ever work at a profit if you needed remote operators anything like that often.
claim: Driverless car companies cannot be commercially viable if they require remote operator interventions as frequently as Cruise does.

quote: Cruise has been presenting its vehicles as autonomous, in hopes that they someday would be, but right now it looks like they are anything but.
claim: Cruise's vehicles are not truly autonomous; they rely heavily on remote human operators despite being presented as autonomous.

quote: it's starting to feel like if the cars were actually left to their own devices, all hell would break loose.
claim: Cruise's driverless cars would likely fail badly if operated without remote human intervention.

quote: remote operators could well be "the dark secret of ALL self-driving."
claim: Heavy reliance on remote operators may be a hidden dependency across the entire driverless car industry, not just Cruise.

quote: The State of California should demand answers immediately, and share them with the public.
claim: Regulators should require public disclosure of how all driverless car companies use remote operators.

quote: Turns out Cruise and (per rumors) others have really been working what we should call "remotely-assisted" driving: the car does *some* of the work, but frequently calls a call-center (aka remote op center). That's basically just driver assist, but with the assistant outside the car, rather than inside.
claim: Cruise and likely other driverless car companies are doing remotely-assisted driving (frequent call-center support), not true autonomous driving.

quote: This is not true autonomy, but semi-autonomy. It might (I doubt it) save money, but it has literally nothing to do with AGI.
claim: Remotely-assisted driving is semi-autonomy, not true autonomy, and has nothing to do with AGI progress.

quote: I don't know if anyone has EVER published clean, detailed remote-assist free data on self-driving, ever, because regulators were not demanding info on remote assist.
claim: No driverless car company has ever published data on vehicle performance without remote human assistance.

quote: If all the "self-driving" efforts are leaning heavily (eg hourly) on call-centers, true self-driving is a LONG way away. Anyone who thinks we are close to true Level 5 self-driving has probably been deceived.
claim: True Level 5 self-driving is a long way away; heavy reliance on remote call centers shows the industry is far from real autonomy.

quote: Essentially NONE of the comparisons we have heard between humans and machines have looked at cars in which humans were not playing some role in the driving, either in the car or remotely.
claim: Published safety comparisons between human and autonomous driving are misleading because they include significant hidden human assistance.

quote: Remote driving itself is unlikely to work well enough as an endgame on public roads, because data communications just aren't stable enough. (Latency and loss of communications are both serious problems)
claim: Remote driving cannot be a viable endgame for public roads because data communication latency and reliability are insufficient.

quote: Uber and Lyft can stop worrying about being disintermediated by machines; they will still need human drivers for quite some time.
claim: Uber and Lyft will still need human drivers for a long time; autonomous vehicles will not disintermediate them soon.

quote: The whole self-driving cars industry is this likely to be viewed in a few years as an epic fail, crushed by outliers and AI that could not reason adequately.
claim: The self-driving car industry will likely be viewed as an epic failure within a few years, due to AI's inability to handle outlier situations and reason adequately.

quote: Driver-assist (a la Tesla) with humans in the loop may well stay, occasionally causing fatal accidents, given its lack of reliability, but the idea of using your car moonlighting as a self-driving taxi is not happening this decade, maybe not even next.
claim: Tesla's vision of cars moonlighting as self-driving taxis will not happen this decade, and possibly not next decade either.
horizon_value: this decade, maybe not even next

quote: We may see similar disappointments in safety-critical applications of LLMs (eg medicine), years of promise, but humans still for a very long time required in the loop, true autonomy difficult to achieve.
claim: Safety-critical LLM applications like medicine will disappoint similarly to self-driving cars, with humans required in the loop for a very long time.

quote: Transformers (the key technology behind LLMs) won't get us to Level 5 self-driving; hope springs eternal, but the fact is that LLMs hallucinate a lot. High-reliability is not their forte. and nor are outliers.
claim: Transformer-based LLMs will not achieve Level 5 self-driving because they hallucinate too much and cannot handle outlier situations reliably.

quote: If true self-driving is this hard, despite the amount of data that has been collected and amount of money invested ($100B+), any dreams of AGI happening soon are likely wildly unrealistic.
claim: The difficulty of achieving true self-driving despite $100B+ investment suggests that AGI arriving soon is wildly unrealistic.

quote: No fantasy about AGI thus far has survived contact with the real world.
claim: No AGI prediction or vision has survived contact with real-world implementation challenges.

trigger: France and Germany blocking EU AI Act
quote: the draft EU AI Act—the closest thing we have to AI regulation with real teeth—is in serious jeopardy, potentially a direct victim of regulatory capture, governments doing the bidding of those they are supposed govern.
claim: The EU AI Act is in jeopardy due to regulatory capture by AI companies influencing France and Germany.

trigger: France and Germany blocking EU AI Act
quote: Literally every bad thing that comes out of generative AI-and there will may well be a lot—people will wonder: "maybe this wouldn't have happened had Macron and Scholz not blocked the planet's best effort at keeping the tech companies in check."
claim: Blocking the EU AI Act will lead to preventable harms from generative AI that regulation could have mitigated.
target: EU AI Act / generative AI harms

quote: here we are 20 months later and in some core sense not a lot has changed; hallucinations are still rampant, large language models still make a lot of ridiculous errors and so forth.
claim: Twenty months after GPT-4's launch, hallucinations remain rampant and LLMs still make many ridiculous errors; not much has fundamentally changed.

quote: people have little by little started to recognize that scoring better on benchmarks is NOT the same as making foundational progress.
claim: Better benchmark scores for LLMs do not equate to foundational progress in AI.

quote: Bill Gates, who not that long said that GPT-4 was a revolution, recently that he did not expect GPT-5 to be that much better than GPT-4. Coming from Gates, this is a big deal, since Gates probably has access to drafts of GPT-5, given that he still owns a lot of Microsoft which owns a lot of OpenAI.
claim: Bill Gates's statement that GPT-5 will not be much better than GPT-4 is significant because Gates likely has insider access to GPT-5 development through Microsoft's stake in OpenAI.

quote: The sooner we stop climbing the hill we are on, and start looking for new paradigms, the better.
claim: AI research needs to move beyond current deep learning paradigms to make real progress.

quote: I had the temerity to argue, right in the middle of an AI explosion, that (a) scale was not everything; (ii) we had no serious solution to problems of compositionality (understanding whole in terms of their parts); (iii) hallucinations were not going away; (iv) reliability would continue to be a problem; (v) misinformation and factuality were unsolved and unlikely to be solved soon; and (vi) large language models would not get us to AGI and that we needed a new paradigm.
claim: Scale alone is insufficient for AI progress; compositionality, hallucinations, reliability, and factuality remain unsolved problems, and LLMs will not achieve AGI without a new paradigm.

quote: The fact that the governance of one of the most visible AI companies in the world can change literally overnight should be a reminder that we can't make our judgements about a company's trustworthiness based simply on a vibe about their CEO.
claim: AI company trustworthiness cannot be assessed based on the perceived character of the CEO; governance can change overnight.

quote: If Sam does come back, they have accomplished nothing (and probably lost Ilya and the $86B secondary sale) and returned to someone they don't trust, and given him more power, which may inherently undermine safety.
claim: If Altman returns to OpenAI after being fired, he will have more power and less oversight, which will undermine AI safety.

quote: By turning to a single corporate entity, Microsoft, for the majority of the cash and computing power OpenAI needed to achieve its mission, it was essentially handling control to Microsoft, even if that control wasn't codified in any formal governance mechanism.
claim: OpenAI's dependence on Microsoft for funding and compute effectively handed control to Microsoft despite the nonprofit governance structure.

quote: those with a stake in the nominally subordinate for-profit (both employees and investors) quickly set to work to push out the board and to undo its decisions.
claim: OpenAI's for-profit stakeholders (employees and investors) successfully overpowered the nonprofit board's governance decisions, undermining the original safety-first mission.

quote: Three orgs filled with brilliant minds tried to create AI independently of the tech giants, and all three have been subverted.
claim: All three major attempts to create AI organizations independent of big tech (OpenAI, DeepMind, Inflection) have been captured or subverted by large tech companies.

quote: If you think that OpenAI has a shot, eventually, at AGI, none of this bodes particularly well.
claim: The collapse of OpenAI's nonprofit governance structure is bad for the prospect of safely developing AGI.

quote: The schisms on display there highlight the fact that we cannot rely purely on the companies to self-regulate AI, wherein even their own internal governance can be deeply conflicted.
claim: The OpenAI leadership crisis demonstrates that AI companies cannot be relied upon to self-regulate, as even their internal governance can be deeply conflicted.

quote: Please don't gut the EU AI Act; we need it now more than ever.
claim: The EU AI Act is essential and should not be weakened, especially given evidence that AI companies cannot self-govern.

quote: GPT's "lies" are also legendary, and prolific, but not really lies, because real lies intend to deceive, and GPT has no intent.
claim: GPT's factual errors are not lies because GPT lacks intent to deceive; they are a structural byproduct of autocompletion over training data.

quote: It autocompletes sentences, some of those completions are true given that the training data is so vast, and some aren't, given that the training data aren't infinite.
claim: GPT produces both true and false statements because it autocompletes based on vast but not infinite training data, without understanding truth.

quote: This is inconsistent with all narratives about "cloddish" or self-interested boards, and suggests that something real happened.
claim: Altman and Brockman giving up their board seats and agreeing to an investigation suggests the OpenAI board had substantive concerns, not just petty infighting.

quote: Humans are very good at a bunch of things that AI is (as of today) still pretty poor at: maintaining cognitive models of the world, inferring semantics from language, comprehending scenes, navigating 3D world, being cognitively flexible
claim: Current AI systems are still poor at maintaining cognitive models of the world, inferring semantics from language, comprehending scenes, navigating the 3D world, and being cognitively flexible compared to humans.

quote: The brutal truth is that you can't reason reliably if you can't maintain cognitive models of the world, no matter how much memory or compute you might have.
claim: Reliable reasoning requires maintaining cognitive models of the world, and cannot be achieved through memory or compute alone.

quote: The secret to making AGI almost certainly rests on getting disparate algorithms (e.g., those that work well with symbolic knowledge, those that can induce regularities from massive amounts of unstructured data, etc) to work together well.
claim: AGI will require integrating disparate algorithmic approaches, including symbolic knowledge systems and statistical learning from unstructured data.

quote: The point of AI should not be to replicate us, but to supplement us, where we are cognitively frail
claim: AI should aim to supplement human cognition rather than replicate it.

quote: AI definitely should not aspire to replicate the human mind in every detail. We already have 8 billion human minds, many pretty problematic; we shouldn't aspire for more of the same.
claim: AI should not aim to replicate the human mind in every detail.

quote: But "breakthroughs" rarely turn to be general to live up to initial rosy expectations. Often advances work in some contexts, not otherwise.
claim: Claimed AI breakthroughs rarely turn out to be general enough to live up to initial expectations; they typically work in some contexts but not others.

quote: Arguably every putative breakthrough in driverless cars has been of this sort; somebody finds something new, it looks good at first, maybe even helps a bit, but at the end of the day, "autonomous" cars still aren't reliable enough for prime time
claim: Every claimed breakthrough in autonomous vehicles has failed to deliver general reliability for real-world deployment.

quote: We are probably still many "breakthroughs" away from true level 5 driverless cars.
claim: True Level 5 autonomous driving is still many breakthroughs away.

quote: I am pretty skeptical
claim: OpenAI's Q* is unlikely to be a fundamental breakthrough that changes the world.

quote: there is no moat. Even if you figure that 750 engineers are worth $10 million each, that only gets you to $7.5b, not $86B.
claim: OpenAI has no significant competitive moat; its technology can be replicated by competitors.

quote: Elon Musk's Grok replicated much (not all) of what OpenAI had done in a few months; Kai-Fu Lee's new company similarly was able to replicate a lot in less than a year.
claim: OpenAI's core technology is replicable, as demonstrated by Grok and Kai-Fu Lee's company reproducing much of it in months.

quote: There has never been a solution to the hallucination problem, and it's not clear that there will be until we find a new paradigm
claim: The hallucination problem in LLMs has no solution within the current paradigm and may require a fundamentally new approach.

quote: There's still no clear business model, and systems like GPT-4 still cost a lot to run.
claim: Generative AI companies like OpenAI lack a clear sustainable business model due to high operating costs.

quote: a lot of companies spent money testing LLMs but few were actually using the LLMs in production. That income is mostly 'proof of concept' testing, but that income could dry up.
claim: Most enterprise LLM revenue comes from proof-of-concept testing rather than production use, and this revenue could dry up.

quote: Large language models alone were never going to solve the alignment problem.
claim: Large language models alone are insufficient to solve the AI alignment problem.

quote: OpenAI's strength has been in scaling up an existing paradigm (Transformers) that was developed elsewhere (Google), not developing new paradigms.
claim: OpenAI's strength is scaling existing paradigms rather than developing new ones; the Transformer architecture was developed at Google.

quote: Our results support the hypothesis that GPT-4, perhaps the most capable "general" LLM currently available, is still not able to robustly form abstractions and reason about basic core-concepts in contexts not previously seen in its training data.
claim: GPT-4 cannot robustly form abstractions and reason about core concepts in novel contexts not seen in its training data.

quote: Without reliable reasoning, how can you get to alignment? Or even reliable software with anything like formal guarantees?
claim: AI alignment and reliable software with formal guarantees require reliable reasoning capabilities that current LLMs lack.

quote: Hinton doesn't have a real argument on understanding, just an intuition.
claim: Hinton lacks a rigorous argument that current deep learning systems understand; he relies on intuition.

quote: On regulation and AI risk, he's the one without a plausible argument, just a hope that everything turn out alright.
claim: LeCun lacks a plausible argument against AI regulation and risk; his position amounts to hoping things turn out fine.

quote: I submit that we are already part way there.
claim: We are already partway to a 'technopolar' world in which Big Tech companies rather than nations make critical governance decisions.

quote: What we should not have is a couple of tech lords sitting around deciding for us. But that's exactly what's happening
claim: Decisions about whether to open-source powerful AI are currently being made by a few tech executives rather than through democratic public processes.

quote: open-source AI might, for example, be used to design bioweapons
claim: Open-source AI could be used to design bioweapons.

quote: If severe risks from foundation models aren't mitigated at the foundation model level, they won't be mitigated at all, potentially threatening the safety of millions of people.
claim: Risks from foundation models can only be effectively mitigated at the foundation model level, not by downstream deployers alone.

quote: Self-regulation is likely to dramatically fall short of the standards required for foundation model safety.
claim: Self-regulation by AI companies will be insufficient to ensure foundation model safety.

quote: The safety of foundation models must be ensured by law.
claim: Foundation model safety must be ensured by law, not voluntary self-regulation.

quote: If the EU does not find a compromise, we are all in trouble; the only beneficiaries will be the largest companies who are least in need of help.
claim: Failure to regulate foundation models in the EU AI Act would primarily benefit the largest AI companies at the expense of everyone else.

quote: Gemini seems to have by many measures matched (or slightly exceeded) GPT-4, but not to have blown it away.
claim: Google's Gemini matched or slightly exceeded GPT-4 on many measures but did not represent a major leap beyond it.

quote: From a commercial standpoint GPT-4 is no longer unique. That's a huge problem for OpenAI, especially post drama, when many customers are now seeking a backup plan.
claim: GPT-4 losing its uniqueness with Gemini's arrival is a major commercial problem for OpenAI.

quote: From a technical standpoint, the key question is: are LLMs close to a plateau?
claim: LLMs may be approaching a performance plateau.

quote: Note that Gates and Altman have both been dropping hints, and GPT-5 isn't here after a year despite immense commercial desire. The fact that Google, with all its resources, did NOT blow away GPT-4 could be telling.
claim: The absence of GPT-5 after a year and Google's inability to dramatically surpass GPT-4 with Gemini suggest LLMs may be plateauing.

quote: The more that comes out, the less likely any of these three theories seems to be correct.
claim: The OpenAI board's concerns about Sam Altman's candor were likely legitimate, not driven by D'Angelo's conflict of interest, board incompetence, or political conspiracy.

quote: while Altman to my knowledge holds no direct equity in OpenAI, he does have an indirect stake in OpenAI, and that fact should have been disclosed.
claim: Sam Altman has an indirect financial stake in OpenAI through Y Combinator that he failed to disclose when testifying to the Senate.

quote: By any reasonable measure they have strayed, very far, from that mission. That's primarily on Sam.
claim: OpenAI has strayed far from its original nonprofit mission of building safe AI for the broad benefit of humanity, primarily due to Sam Altman's leadership.

quote: What we are going to see, in the fullness of time, I promise you, is that Gemini is more or less in the same ball park as GPT-4, handy for a bunch of things, but untethered in reality, still with dicey, unpredictable reasoning, and a very limited understanding of the world.
claim: Google Gemini is roughly comparable to GPT-4 and shares the same fundamental limitations: unreliable reasoning and limited understanding of the world.

quote: The video is almost never the true story.
claim: AI demo videos from tech companies almost never accurately represent the true capabilities of the product.

trigger: Altman claiming GPT-4 passes Turing Test
quote: ELIZA (built in 1965-1966) beat GPT 3.5. That's embarrassing!
claim: ELIZA, a 1966 chatbot, performed competitively with GPT-3.5 on the Turing Test, undermining the test's usefulness as a measure of AI intelligence.

trigger: Altman claiming GPT-4 passes Turing Test
quote: Dubious Google Gemini videos aside, nobody is close to passing that yet.
claim: No current AI system is close to passing Marcus's proposed Comprehension Challenge, which tests genuine language understanding.

quote: The phrase "Added late in the text" — which tries to paint the regulation of foundation models as some kind of sneaky last minute move — is a massive, massive misrepresentation.
claim: Yann LeCun's claim that foundation model regulation was added late to the EU AI Act is a misrepresentation; it was first proposed by the French government in May 2022.

quote: As clever as FunSearch is, it's unlikely to be a major factor in solving cancer or making lightweight batteries.
claim: Google DeepMind's FunSearch is unlikely to generalize to solving major real-world problems like cancer or battery design.

quote: An LLM didn't solve the math problem on its own; the LLM was used in a very narrow, prescribed way inside of larger system.
claim: FunSearch did not demonstrate an LLM solving a math problem independently; the LLM was used in a narrow, prescribed way within a larger engineered system.

quote: Human mathematicians had to write problem-specific code for each separate mathematical problem.
claim: FunSearch required human mathematicians to write problem-specific code for each mathematical problem, limiting its generality.

quote: It is not inconceivable that AI-generated propaganda could be significantly intensifying the scale and impact of such a campaign.
claim: AI-generated propaganda may be significantly intensifying the scale and impact of foreign influence campaigns to destabilize the United States.

quote: The algorithm may be fine when it is directed at dancing bears, but when directed at the daily news, it is likely doing serious damage to our ability to co-exist as humans.
claim: Social media recommendation algorithms are likely doing serious damage to social cohesion when applied to news and political content.

quote: It also suggests that Midjourney has been trained on high-resolution copyright images, to which they may or may not have a license.
claim: Midjourney appears to have been trained on high-resolution copyrighted images, potentially without licensing rights.

quote: a user of Midjourney could easily inadvertently infringe on copyright.
claim: Users of Midjourney can easily and inadvertently produce copyright-infringing outputs.

quote: Generative AI systems like DALL-E and ChatGPT have been trained on copyrighted materials
claim: Generative AI systems like DALL-E and ChatGPT have been trained on copyrighted materials.

quote: Generative AI systems are fully capable of producing materials that infringe on copyright.
claim: Generative AI systems are capable of producing outputs that infringe on copyright.
